subject: Physics
q: A point source is emitting sound waves of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at the origin. The difference in intensity (magnitude only) at two points located at a distances of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the origin respectively will be ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A point source is emitting sound waves of intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at the origin. The difference in intensity (magnitude only) at two points located at a distances of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the origin respectively will be ________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Intensity for a point source varies as I∝1r2.

Intensity at a distance of 2 m, I2=16×10-8×122=4×10-8 W m-2

Similarly, intensity at a distance of 4 m, I4=16×10-8×142=1×10-8 W m-2

Required difference, I2-I4=3×10-8 W m-2

subject: Physics
q: Two open organ pipes of lengths $60 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $90 \mathrm{~cm}$ resonate at $6^{\text {th }}$ and $5^{\text {th }}$ harmonics respectively. The difference of frequencies for the given modes is _______ $\mathrm{Hz}$. (Velocity of sound in air $=333 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )
question: Two open organ pipes of lengths $60 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $90 \mathrm{~cm}$ resonate at $6^{\text {th }}$ and $5^{\text {th }}$ harmonics respectively. The difference of frequencies for the given modes is _______ $\mathrm{Hz}$. (Velocity of sound in air $=333 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )
solution:
The difference in frequency in open organ pipe $=$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{f}=\frac{\mathrm{nv}}{2 \mathrm{~L}} \\
& \Delta \mathrm{f}=\frac{6 \mathrm{v}}{2 \times 0.6}-\frac{5 \mathrm{v}}{2 \times 0.9} \\
& \mathrm{v}=333 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \Delta \mathrm{f}=740 \mathrm{~Hz}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Two cars A and B are moving away from each other in opposite directions. Both the cars are moving with speed of 20 m s-1 with respect to the ground. If an observer in car A detects a frequency 2000 Hz of the sound coming from car B, what is the natural frequency of the sound source in car B?
(speed of sound in air =340 m s-1 )
question:
Two cars A and B are moving away from each other in opposite directions. Both the cars are moving with speed of 20 m s-1 with respect to the ground. If an observer in car A detects a frequency 2000 Hz of the sound coming from car B, what is the natural frequency of the sound source in car B?
(speed of sound in air =340 m s-1 )
solution:
From Doppler effect: f'=V∓ V0V±VSf0
2000=340-20340+20f0=320360f0
f0=98×2000=2250 Hz

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following equations represents a travelling wave?
question: Which of the following equations represents a travelling wave?
solution:
The given function should be in the form of fx±vt.

So, for travelling wave y should be linear function of x and tand should be finite and continuous for all values of t. 

y=Asin15x-2t→ linear function in x and t.

subject: Physics
q: A car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> travelling at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> sounds its horn at a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. Another car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is travelling behind the first car in the same direction with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The frequency heard by the passenger of the car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is approximately [Take, velocity of sound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>360</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: A car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> travelling at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> sounds its horn at a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. Another car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is travelling behind the first car in the same direction with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The frequency heard by the passenger of the car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is approximately [Take, velocity of sound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>360</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Let velocity of sound in air be v m s-1. Using Doppler formula,

f=f0v+vov-vs=f0v+40v-20
=400360+40340
≈471 Hz

subject: Physics
q: The distance between two consecutive points with phase difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> in a wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The velocity with which wave is travelling is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: The distance between two consecutive points with phase difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> in a wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The velocity with which wave is travelling is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Distance between two consecutive points 60° out of phase is given by Δx=λ2πΔϕ

Putting the values, we have

6=λ2ππ3

⇒λ=36 m

Now, velocity of wave is v=fλ=(500 Hz)(36 m)

=18000 m s-1=18 km s-1

subject: Physics
q: A granite rod of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length is clamped at its middle point and is set into longitudinal vibrations. The density of granite is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi> </mi><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and its Young's modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9.27</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mi> </mi><mi>Pa</mi></math>. What will be the fundamental frequency of the longitudinal vibrations?
question: A granite rod of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length is clamped at its middle point and is set into longitudinal vibrations. The density of granite is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi> </mi><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and its Young's modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9.27</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mi> </mi><mi>Pa</mi></math>. What will be the fundamental frequency of the longitudinal vibrations?
solution:
L=⋋2⇒⋋=2L=1.2 m

v=yρ=5.86×103 m s-1

∴ Fundamental frequency=v⋋=4.88 kHz

≈5 kHz

subject: Physics
q: A tuning fork arrangement (pair) produces 4 beats / sec with one fork of frequency $288 \mathrm{cps}$. A little wax is placed on the unknown fork and it then produces 2 beats /sec. The frequency of the unknown fork is
question: A tuning fork arrangement (pair) produces 4 beats / sec with one fork of frequency $288 \mathrm{cps}$. A little wax is placed on the unknown fork and it then produces 2 beats /sec. The frequency of the unknown fork is
solution:
The fact that placing wax decreases the frequency of the unknown fork and also the beat frequency states that the unknown fork is of higher frequency.
$$
n-288=4 \Rightarrow n=292 \mathrm{cps}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A sinusoidal wave of wavelength 7.5 cm travels a distance of 1.2 cm along the x -direction in 0.3 sec. The crest $P$ is at $x=0$ at $t=0 \mathrm{sec}$ and maximum displacement of the wave is 2 cm. Which equation correctly represents this wave ?
question: A sinusoidal wave of wavelength 7.5 cm travels a distance of 1.2 cm along the x -direction in 0.3 sec. The crest $P$ is at $x=0$ at $t=0 \mathrm{sec}$ and maximum displacement of the wave is 2 cm. Which equation correctly represents this wave ?
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{v}=\frac{\text { distance }}{\text { time }} \\ & \mathrm{v}=\frac{12}{0.3}=4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{k}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=\frac{2 \pi}{7.5}=\frac{4 \pi}{15}=0.83 \\ & \mathrm{v}=\frac{\omega}{\mathrm{k}} \Rightarrow \omega=\mathrm{vk}=4 \times \frac{4 \pi}{15}=3.35
\end{aligned}$
So $y=A \cos (k x-\omega t)$

subject: Physics
q: A source and a detector move away from each other in absence of wind with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, with respect to the ground. If the detector detects a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> of the sound coming from the source, then the original frequency of source considering the speed of sound in the air <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>340</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math>.
question: A source and a detector move away from each other in absence of wind with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, with respect to the ground. If the detector detects a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> of the sound coming from the source, then the original frequency of source considering the speed of sound in the air <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>340</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math>.
solution:
The formula of apparent frequency in terms of velocity of the source and observer is, f′=fv−v0v+vs, now put the values which are given in the question, 

⇒1800=f340−20340+20⇒f=1800×360320 Hz⇒f=2025 Hz

subject: Physics
q: Two sitar strings, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> playing the note '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi><mi>h</mi><mi>a</mi></math>' are slightly out of tune and produce beats of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The tension of the string <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is slightly increased and the beat frequency is found to decrease by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. If the frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>425</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The original frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is
question: Two sitar strings, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> playing the note '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi><mi>h</mi><mi>a</mi></math>' are slightly out of tune and produce beats of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The tension of the string <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is slightly increased and the beat frequency is found to decrease by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. If the frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>425</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. The original frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is
solution: The frequency of B is either 420 Hz or 430 Hz. As, the tension in B is increased its frequency will increase. If the frequency is 430 Hz, the beat frequency will increase. If the frequency is 420 Hz beat frequency will decrease, hence the correct answer is 420 Hz.

subject: Physics
q: Two cars are approaching each other at an equal speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>hr</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> When they see each other, both blow horns having a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>676</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The beat frequency heard by each driver will be _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> [Velocity of sound in air is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>340</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
question: Two cars are approaching each other at an equal speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>hr</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> When they see each other, both blow horns having a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>676</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The beat frequency heard by each driver will be _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> [Velocity of sound in air is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>340</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Frequency of sound heard by car-1, which comes by reflection from car-2

f1=f0340+2340-2340+2340-2

=f03423382

Frequency of sound coming directly from car-2

f2=f0340+2340-2

∴  f1-f2=f0342338342338-1=8.09≃8 Hz

subject: Physics
q: A wire of length 2L, is made by joining two wires A and B of same length but different radii r and 2r and made of the same material. It is vibrating at a frequency such that the joint of the two wires forms a node. If the number of antinodes in wire A is p and that in B is q then ratio p:q is:
question: A wire of length 2L, is made by joining two wires A and B of same length but different radii r and 2r and made of the same material. It is vibrating at a frequency such that the joint of the two wires forms a node. If the number of antinodes in wire A is p and that in B is q then ratio p:q is:
solution:
For rod A, f1=n12lTρA1
For rod B, f2=n22lTρA2
Given f1=f2
n12lTρA1=n22lTρA2      
n12lTρA1=n22lTρ(4A1)      ∵rB=2rAA2=4A1
n1=n22
n1n2=12

subject: Physics
q: A uniform tube of length $60.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ is held vertically with its lower end dipped in water. A sound source of frequency $500 \mathrm{~Hz}$ sends sound waves into the tube. When the length of tube above water is $16 \mathrm{~cm}$ and again when it is $50 \mathrm{~cm}$, the tube resonates with the source of sound. Two lowest frequencies (in $\mathrm{Hz}$ ), to which tube will resonate when it is taken out of water, are (approximately).
question: A uniform tube of length $60.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ is held vertically with its lower end dipped in water. A sound source of frequency $500 \mathrm{~Hz}$ sends sound waves into the tube. When the length of tube above water is $16 \mathrm{~cm}$ and again when it is $50 \mathrm{~cm}$, the tube resonates with the source of sound. Two lowest frequencies (in $\mathrm{Hz}$ ), to which tube will resonate when it is taken out of water, are (approximately).
solution: Two lowest frequencies to which tube will resonates are $272 \mathrm{~Hz}$ and $544 \mathrm{~Hz}$.

subject: Physics
q: The equation of a wave travelling on a string is $\mathrm{y}=\sin [20 \pi \mathrm{x}+10 \pi \mathrm{t}]$, where x and t are distance and time in SI units. The minimum distance between two points having the same oscillating speed is :
question: The equation of a wave travelling on a string is $\mathrm{y}=\sin [20 \pi \mathrm{x}+10 \pi \mathrm{t}]$, where x and t are distance and time in SI units. The minimum distance between two points having the same oscillating speed is :
solution:
Minimum distance between 2 points having same speed is $\frac{\lambda}{2}$.
$\lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{~m}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\text { Distance }=\frac{\lambda}{2}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q: Two identical strings <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> made of same material have tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></msub></math> in then if their fundamental frequencies are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>450</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>, respectively, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub><mo>/</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></msub></math> is :
question: Two identical strings <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> made of same material have tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></msub></math> in then if their fundamental frequencies are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>450</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>, respectively, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub><mo>/</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></msub></math> is :
solution:
fX=12ℓTXμ

fZ=12ℓTZμ

fXfZ=450300=TXTZ

⇒TX/TZ=9/4=2.25

subject: Physics
q: A pipe of length 85 cm is closed from one end. Find the number of possible natural oscillations of air column in the pipe whose frequencies lie below 1250 Hz. The velocity of sound in air is 340 m/s.
question: A pipe of length 85 cm is closed from one end. Find the number of possible natural oscillations of air column in the pipe whose frequencies lie below 1250 Hz. The velocity of sound in air is 340 m/s.
solution:
fundamental frequency

       


L=λ4v1=v4L


       


L = λ 2 + λ 4 = 3 λ 4

v2=3v4L

∴   v=oddv4L

Now L = 8 5 cm = 0.85 m

        v=340m/s

          = odd 3 4 0 4 × 0.85

         =odd×100 Hz

Hence possible frequencies below 1250 Hz are

100 Hz, 300 Hz, 500 Hz...........1100 Hz

Hence 6

subject: Physics
q: An observer moves towards a stationary source of sound, with a velocity one fifth of the velocity of sound. What is the percentage increase in the apparent frequency?
question: An observer moves towards a stationary source of sound, with a velocity one fifth of the velocity of sound. What is the percentage increase in the apparent frequency?
solution:
$$
f=\frac{v+v / 5}{v} f=\frac{6 f}{5}
$$
$\%$ increase in frequency $=20 \%$

subject: Physics
q: The frequency of a car horn encountered a change from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. When the car approaches a vertical wall. If the speed of sound is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>330</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Then the speed of car is ___ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: The frequency of a car horn encountered a change from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. When the car approaches a vertical wall. If the speed of sound is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>330</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Then the speed of car is ___ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Wall as an observer
Frequency received by wall, 

f1=f0CC-V
Again wall as a source
Frequency received by observer on car, 
f2=f1C+VC
f2=f0C+VC-V
500=400C+VC-V
54=C+VC-V
C=9 V

V=C9=3309 m s-1
V=3309×185=132 km h-1

subject: Physics
q: Two engines pass each other moving in opposite directions with uniform speed of 30 m/s. one of them is blowing a whistle of frequency 540 Hz. Calculate the frequency heard by driver of second engine before they pass each other. speed of sound is 330 m/sec:
question: Two engines pass each other moving in opposite directions with uniform speed of 30 m/s. one of them is blowing a whistle of frequency 540 Hz. Calculate the frequency heard by driver of second engine before they pass each other. speed of sound is 330 m/sec:
solution:
From Doppler's effect of sound, apparent frequency is given by, 
f′=v+v0v-vsf, where v, vo, vs&f represents velocity of sound, velocity of observer, velocity of source and frequency of source repectively.
=330+30330-30×540
=360300×540
=648 Hz

subject: Physics
q: The velocity of sound in air is doubled when the temperature is raised from $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $\alpha^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: The velocity of sound in air is doubled when the temperature is raised from $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $\alpha^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
The velocity of sound in air depends on temperature as $v \propto \sqrt{T}$ (absolute temperature).
If the velocity doubles:
$\frac{v_2}{v_1} = 2 = \sqrt{\frac{T_2}{T_1}}$.
Squaring: $\frac{T_2}{T_1} = 4$.
With $T_1 = 0°C = 273.15$ K and $T_2 = 273.15 + \alpha$ K:
$\frac{273.15 + \alpha}{273.15} = 4$, giving $273.15 + \alpha = 1092.6$ K.
Therefore $\alpha = 819.45°C \approx 819°C$.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as
Reason $\mathbf{R}$
Assertion A: A sound wave has higher speed in solids than gases.
Reason R: Gases have higher value of Bulk modulus than solids.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as
Reason $\mathbf{R}$
Assertion A: A sound wave has higher speed in solids than gases.
Reason R: Gases have higher value of Bulk modulus than solids.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are true but $\mathbf{R}$ is NOT the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$
Both $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are true and $\mathbf{R}$ is the correct explanation of $\mathbf{A}$
solution:
Speed of sound in a medium depends on inertial and elastic properties as $v=\sqrt{\frac{B}{P}}$ for gases and $v=\sqrt{\frac{Y}{P}}$ for solids. Since the elastic property of solid happens to be many folds greater than that of gases, the speed of sound in solids i5 higher than in gases.

Also, bulk modulus of gases varies between 0 and $\infty\left(B=-v \frac{d P}{d v}\right)$ hence reason is false.

subject: Physics
q: Two harmonic waves moving in the same direction superimpose to form a wave $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a} \cos (1.5 \mathrm{t}) \cos (50.5 \mathrm{t})$ where $t$ is in seconds. Find the period with which they beat (close to nearest integer)
question: Two harmonic waves moving in the same direction superimpose to form a wave $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a} \cos (1.5 \mathrm{t}) \cos (50.5 \mathrm{t})$ where $t$ is in seconds. Find the period with which they beat (close to nearest integer)
solution:
The given equation can be written as
$\mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2} \cos [1.5+50.5] \mathrm{t}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2} \cos [50.5-1.5]$
$x=\frac{a}{2} \cos [52 t]+\frac{a}{2} \cos [49 t]$
Here, $2 \pi \mathrm{f}_1 \& 2 \pi \mathrm{f}_2=49$
$\mathrm{f}_1=\frac{52}{2 \pi}, \mathrm{f}_2=\frac{49}{2 \pi}$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}_{\text {Bat }}=\mathrm{f}_1-\mathrm{f}_2=\frac{3}{2 \pi} \mathrm{~Hz}$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}_{\text {Beat }}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\text {Beat }}}=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \mathrm{sec}$
$=2.09 \mathrm{sec} \approx 2 \mathrm{sec}$

subject: Physics
q:
A tunning fork of frequency 340 Hz resonates in the fundamental mode with an air column of length 125 cm in a cylindrical tube closed at one end. When water is slowly poured in it, the minimum height of water required for observing resonance once again is _____ cm.
(Velocity of sound in air is 340 ms-1)
question:
A tunning fork of frequency 340 Hz resonates in the fundamental mode with an air column of length 125 cm in a cylindrical tube closed at one end. When water is slowly poured in it, the minimum height of water required for observing resonance once again is _____ cm.
(Velocity of sound in air is 340 ms-1)
solution:
We know that, v=λf, then, λ=vf=340 m s-1340 Hz=340 m s-1340 s-1=1 m

First resonating length, l1=λ4=1004=25 cm

Second resonating length, l2=3λ4=3×1004=75 cm

Third resonating length, l3=5λ4=5×1004=125 cm

So third resonance is not possible since the length of the tube is 125 cm

Thus, minimum height of water necessary for resonance =125−75=50 cm

q: Two strings with circular cross section and made of same material, are stretched to have same amount of tension. A transverse wave is then made to pass through both the strings. The velocity of the wave in the first string having the radius of cross section R is $\mathrm{v}_1$, and that in the other string having radius of cross section $R / 2$ is $v_2$. Then $\frac{v_2}{v_1}=$
question: Two strings with circular cross section and made of same material, are stretched to have same amount of tension. A transverse wave is then made to pass through both the strings. The velocity of the wave in the first string having the radius of cross section R is $\mathrm{v}_1$, and that in the other string having radius of cross section $R / 2$ is $v_2$. Then $\frac{v_2}{v_1}=$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & v=\sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}=\sqrt{\frac{T}{f \pi R^2}} \\ & \frac{v_2}{v_1}=\frac{R_1}{R_2}=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: For a certain organ pipe, the first three resonance frequencies are in the ratio of 1:3:5 respectively. If the frequency of fifth harmonic is 405 Hz and the speed of sound in air is 324 m s–1 the length of the organ pipe is _____ m.
question: For a certain organ pipe, the first three resonance frequencies are in the ratio of 1:3:5 respectively. If the frequency of fifth harmonic is 405 Hz and the speed of sound in air is 324 m s–1 the length of the organ pipe is _____ m.
solution:
It is given,

f5=405 Hz.

As the first three resonance frequencies are in the ratio of 1:3:5, therefore it must be a closed organ pipe.

The frequency formula in a closed organ pipe is given by f=nv4l. So,

5v4I=405 Hz

⇒I=405×45×324=1 m

question: A transverse wave travels on a taut steel wire with a velocity of <math><mi>v</mi></math> when tension in it is <math><mn>2.06</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When the tension is changed to <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> the velocity changed to <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is close to:
solution:
v∝T
⇒v1v2=T1T2⇒vv2=2.06×104T
⇒T=2.06×1044N=0.515×104N

subject: Physics
q: A source of sound <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> is moving with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> towards a stationary observer. The observer measures the frequency of the source as <math><mn>1000</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What will be the apparent frequency of the source when it is moving away from the observer after crossing him? (Take velocity of sound in air is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi> </mi><mn>350</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
question: A source of sound <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> is moving with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> towards a stationary observer. The observer measures the frequency of the source as <math><mn>1000</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What will be the apparent frequency of the source when it is moving away from the observer after crossing him? (Take velocity of sound in air is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi> </mi><mn>350</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
solution:
When source approaches, f1=f0vv-vs
When source recedes, f2=f0vv+vs
⇒ f2f1=v-vsv+vs
⇒f2=1000×350-50350+50 Hz=750 Hz

question: Two travelling waves produces a standing wave represented by equation. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>cos</mi><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>57</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mn>78</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The node closest to the origin in the region <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> will be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mtext>in</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
At Node,
y=0

⇒cos1.57xsin78.5t=0

⇒cos(1.57x)=0

⇒1.57x=π2

⇒x=1 cm
